title: Colors
description: Learn to name and describe colors in Vietnamese — and discover why 'xanh' is both blue and green.

sections.content:
Vietnamese color words work a bit differently from English. The most important thing to know: the word 'xanh' covers BOTH blue and green. To specify which one, Vietnamese adds a clarifier: 'xanh dương' (blue, literally 'ocean blue') and 'xanh lá' or 'xanh lá cây' (green, literally 'leaf green').

To describe something's color, use the pattern: object + màu + color. For example: 'cái áo màu đỏ' (a red shirt). You can also drop 'màu' in casual speech: 'cái áo đỏ'.

items.notes: Red is the luckiest color in Vietnamese culture. Used for Tết (Lunar New Year) decorations, weddings, and lucky money envelopes (bao lì xì).

exampleSentence.vietnamese: Cái áo dài màu đỏ đẹp quá.
exampleSentence.english: That red áo dài is so beautiful.

items.notes: Literally 'ocean xanh'. 'Xanh dương' or 'xanh da trời' (sky-skin blue) specifies blue. In Southern pronunciation, 'x' sounds like /s/. Without the qualifier, 'xanh' is ambiguous between blue and green.

exampleSentence.vietnamese: Trời hôm nay xanh dương lắm.
exampleSentence.english: The sky today is very blue.

items.notes: Short for 'xanh lá cây' (tree-leaf green). This is how you specify green as opposed to blue. In everyday speech, 'xanh lá' is more common than the full form.

exampleSentence.vietnamese: Rau xanh lá tốt cho sức khỏe.
exampleSentence.english: Green vegetables are good for health.

items.notes: 'Vàng' means both 'yellow' (the color) and 'gold' (the metal/color). Gold is associated with wealth and prosperity.
White is associated with mourning and funerals in Vietnamese culture — the opposite of Western tradition. White headbands are worn at funerals.

exampleSentence.vietnamese: Áo dài trắng là đồng phục của nữ sinh.
exampleSentence.english: The white áo dài is the school uniform for female students.

items.notes: Black can have negative connotations in Vietnamese ('đen' also means 'unlucky' in slang — 'xui đen' = bad luck). But it's also a stylish, neutral color for clothing.

exampleSentence.vietnamese: Con mèo đen dễ thương ghê.
exampleSentence.english: The black cat is so cute.

items.notes: 'Hồng' also means 'rose' (hoa hồng = rose flower). Pink is generally seen as a feminine, gentle color.
Purple is associated with Huế (Central Vietnam) — the old imperial capital is known for its purple theme. 'Huế tím' is a common phrase.

exampleSentence.vietnamese: Bông hoa màu tím đẹp quá.
exampleSentence.english: That purple flower is so pretty.

items.notes: 'Cam' is both the color orange and the orange fruit — just like in English. 'Trái cam' or 'quả cam' = an orange fruit. 'Màu cam' = orange color.

exampleSentence.english: Fresh orange juice is very delicious.

items.notes: Also used in 'cà phê nâu' (brown coffee), which is another way to say coffee with milk in some regions.

exampleSentence.vietnamese: Trời xám quá, chắc sắp mưa.
exampleSentence.english: The sky is so gray, it's probably about to rain.

items.notes: 'Bạc' means both the color silver and the metal silver. 'Tóc bạc' = gray/silver hair (a sign of aging). Also note: 'bạc' can mean 'ungrateful/disloyal' in some contexts.

exampleSentence.english: Grandpa's hair has turned silver already.

sections.content:
Colors carry deep meaning in Vietnamese culture:

• Red (đỏ) — The luckiest color. Used for Tết, weddings, bao lì xì (lucky money envelopes). Brides often wear red.
• Yellow/Gold (vàng) — Prosperity and royalty. Yellow was historically reserved for the emperor. Hoa mai (yellow apricot blossom) is THE Tết flower in the South.
• White (trắng) — Mourning and funerals. Wearing all white to a Vietnamese celebration would be very inappropriate. White headbands and armbands are worn at funerals.
• Black (đen) — Can mean bad luck in colloquial speech ('xui đen'). Not commonly worn to festive events.
• Pink/Red (hồng/đỏ) — Romance and happiness.

For Tết specifically: red + yellow/gold are the dominant colors. You'll see these everywhere during Lunar New Year — on decorations, envelopes, flowers, and outfits.

Describing colors — the patterns:

• Full form: cái + noun + màu + color → 'cái áo màu đỏ' (a red shirt)
• Casual: cái + noun + color → 'cái áo đỏ' (a red shirt)
• Asking: 'Cái này màu gì?' (What color is this?)
• Saying your favorite: 'Con thích màu ___' (I like the color ___)

Remember: 'xanh' alone is ambiguous — always add 'dương' (blue) or 'lá' (green) to be specific.

exercises.question: What is the difference between 'xanh dương' and 'xanh lá'?
options: xanh dương = green, xanh lá = blue
xanh dương = blue, xanh lá = green
exercises.correctAnswer: xanh dương = blue, xanh lá = green
exercises.explanation: 'Xanh dương' (ocean xanh) = blue. 'Xanh lá' (leaf xanh) = green. Vietnamese uses 'xanh' for both blue and green, so you need a qualifier to be specific.

exercises.question: Which color is considered unlucky and associated with funerals in Vietnamese culture?
exercises.explanation: White is the color of mourning in Vietnamese culture. White headbands are worn at funerals. This is the opposite of Western culture, where black is the mourning color.

exercises.question: How do you ask 'What color is this?' in Vietnamese? Type: Cái này màu gì?
exercises.explanation: 'Cái này' = this thing, 'màu' = color, 'gì' = what. 'Cái này màu gì?' = What color is this?

exercises.question: Which flower is yellow and represents Tết (Lunar New Year) in Southern Vietnam?
exercises.explanation: Hoa mai (yellow apricot blossom) is THE Tết flower in Southern Vietnam. In the North, hoa đào (pink peach blossom) is the Tết flower instead.

exercises.question: Type 'I like the color red' in Vietnamese (speaking as a child to parents):
exercises.explanation: 'Con' = I (child to parent), 'thích' = like, 'màu' = color, 'đỏ' = red.
